coredata.dc:title: Theoretical studies on the feasibility of the hybrid nanocomposites of graphene quantum dot and phenoxazine-based dyes as an efficient sensitizer for dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Feng
Yang, Chuan-Lu
Wang, Mei-Shan
Ma, Xiao-Guang
Liu, Wen-Wang
coredata.dc:description: The feasibility of the hybrid nanocomposites of the graphene quantum dot (GQD) and the phenoxazine-based dyes as the efficient sensitizer of the dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) is investigated. Based on the first principles density functional theory (DFT), the geometrical structures of the separate GQDs, the phenoxazine-based dyes, and their hybridized nanocomposites are fully optimized. The energy stabilities of the obtained structures are confirmed by harmonic frequency analysis. The optical absorptions of the optimized structures are calculated with the time-dependent DFT (TDDFT). The feasibility of the nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC is examined by the charge spatial separation, the molecular orbital energy levels of the nanocomposites and the I−/I3 − electrolyte, and the conduction band minimum of TiO2 electrode. The results demonstrate that three of the eight considered nanocomposites satisfy the requirement of DSSC. Among them, GQD4-POXB with large LHE, high V oc, and enhancement absorption becomes the most promising candidate as a feasible sensitizer. These findings are helpful for the design of the sensitizer of DSSC or the solar energy harvesting materials with the nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Charge spatial separation
Optical absorption
Graphene quantum dot
Phenoxazine-based byes
Nanocomposites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Geometrical Structures and Vibrational Spectra of QDs the Nanocomposites
Feasibility of Nanocomposites as Sensitizers of DSSC

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant No. 11574125 and No. NSFC-11374132 , as well as the Taishan scholar project of Shandong Province ( ts201511055 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimized structures for GQD1(C54H18), GQD2(C66H20), GQD3(C80H22), GQD4(C96H24), POXB, and POXP.
The optimized structures for nanocomposites of GQD1, GQD2, GQD3, and GQD4 hybridized POXB or POXP.
The vibrational spectra for the nanocomposites of GQDX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4)-POXB and GQDX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4)-POXP, calculated at B3LYP/6–31 + G(d)/PCM-THF.

CBM of TiO2 and I−/I3 − represents the electrolyte and energy levels of HOMO and LUMO for GQDX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4)-POXB and GQDX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4)-POXP nanocomposites, calculated at B3LYP/6–31 + G(d)/PCM-THF.
HOMO and LUMO of GQDX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4)-POXB and GQDX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4)-POXP nanocomposites, calculated at the B3LYP/6–31 + G(d)/PCM-THF.
The absorption spectra of GQDX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4)-POXB and GQDX (X = 1, 2, 3, 4)-POXP nanocomposites, calculated by PCM-TD-CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) level of theory in THF.
Calculated main electron transitions, maximum absorption wavelengths (λ max), oscillator strengths (f), and excitation energies of the nanocomposites in THF solvent at TD-PCM-CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d).
Calculated E ox nan∗ , ΔG injec, LHE, and V oc of the nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Theoretical studies on the feasibility of the hybrid nanocomposites of graphene quantum dot and phenoxazine-based dyes as an efficient sensitizer for dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronics Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronics Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The feasibility of the hybrid nanocomposites of the graphene quantum dot (GQD) and the phenoxazine-based dyes as the efficient sensitizer of the dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) is investigated. Based on the first principles density functional theory (DFT), the geometrical structures of the separate GQDs, the phenoxazine-based dyes, and their hybridized nanocomposites are fully optimized. The energy stabilities of the obtained structures are confirmed by harmonic frequency analysis. The optical absorptions of the optimized structures are calculated with the time-dependent DFT (TDDFT). The feasibility of the nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC is examined by the charge spatial separation, the molecular orbital energy levels of the nanocomposites and the I−/I3 − electrolyte, and the conduction band minimum of TiO2 electrode. The results demonstrate that three of the eight considered nanocomposites satisfy the requirement of DSSC. Among them, GQD4-POXB with large LHE, high V oc, and enhancement absorption becomes the most promising candidate as a feasible sensitizer. These findings are helpful for the design of the sensitizer of DSSC or the solar energy harvesting materials with the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, searching for renewable and environmentally friendly energy sources has become a global interest motif due to the long-term shortage issues of fossil fuels and serious environmental issues. The abundance of energy coming from the sun makes solar cells a very attractive alternative for the major sources used today. With the potential of becoming a clean and renewable energy source, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) have drawn much attention because of their relatively high photoelectric absorption efficiencies and lower production cost. A typical DSSC consists of a wide band gap semiconductor photoanode, an anchored molecular sensitizer, a redox electrolyte and a counter electrode [1–3]. In these components, the sensitizer dye plays a vital role in the light-harvesting efficiency and an efficient sensitizer is needed to harvest the solar energy and to inject electrons in DSSC. The development of new sensitizer for DSSC is very important to improve the overall efficiency of the cell through a better understanding about the function of the cell and its limitations. In recent years, many theoretical and experimental studies have focused on designing novel sensitizer for improving DSSC performance [4]. Among the wide range of organic dyes being investigated for application in DSSC, phenoxazine-based sensitizer could be a hopeful sensitizer in DSSC due to the satisfactory electrochemical characteristics [5,6]. Phenoxazine dyes have been used in other applications, such as laser dyes [7], hole-transporting materials [8], and host-guest systems [9]. Despite phenoxazine-based sensitizer potential for application to DSSC, some structures of them do not have very strong absorption in the visible light region, thus they have not led the high-efficient DSSC. Recently, Chermahini et al. [10] have reported a series of phenoxazine-based dyes. The results show that the dye with a furan spacer (POXF) is the most efficient sensitizer for DSSC due to the largest light-harvesting efficiency, high open-circuit photovoltage and red-shifted absorption. However, the optical absorption of some phenoxazine-based dyes is not obvious in the visible light range. Such as the phenoxazine as the electron donor and cyanoacrylic acid as the electron acceptor groups, and benzene (POXB) and pyrrole (POXP) act as the π-spacers. Although the POXB and POXP satisfy the requirement of the energy level as a sensitizer, they are not the efficient sensitizer of DSSC because they lack strong absorption in the most part of the visible light region. The satisfactory efficient solar cell sensitizer to harvest solar energy should have strong absorption in the range from UV to infrared wavelength of the sun. Therefore, to make POXB and POXP become more effective sensitizer, one should enhance the optical absorption of these structures in the visible light range by modifying the structures with some functional ligands or hybridizing quantum dots (QDs). In fact, the dye sensitizer consisting of many nanocomposites prove to be efficient to harvest solar energy, because the nanocomposites can have superior properties as compared to the individual components from which it is made [11,12]. Among the various QDs, graphene quantum dot (GQD) demonstrated most of the excellent features and optical properties, which make them a potential candidate for the fabrication of nanocomposites with favorable properties. Unlike the bulk graphene, GQD has a band gap because of quantum confinement [13–15] and exhibit strong photoluminescence (PL) [16–18]. Nowadays, GQD has become a multifaceted candidate material for next-generation ultrathin optoelectronic devices, and building blocks of the future electronics devices [19–21]. The applications of GQD in renewable energy resources like photovoltaics [22], in electronics and optoelectronics such as photodetector [23] and phototransistor [24], have already been demonstrated by an enormous amount of investigations. By varying the size of GQD, one can tune the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of GQD over a wide range of wavelengths from the ultraviolet through the visible to the infrared light range, which has been confirmed by Mahasin et al. [25] from the experiment which the PL of GQD can be sensitively tuned by controlling its size, shape, edge configuration, chemical functionalization and doping heteroatoms.

Consequently, many attempts have been made for the different size GQDs sensitized composite materials in order to enhance the efficiency along with simultaneous improvements in the performance of DSSC. In a very recent article, Mandal et al. [26] performed a theoretical investigation on the electronic structure of porphyrin functionalized GQDs. The possibility of forming type-II nanohybrids is explored by varying either the size of the GQD or by attaching different functional groups to the porphyrin moiety. Considering that POXB and POXP satisfy the energy level requirement as a sensitizer for DSSC but their light absorptions are very poor in the light visible region, we construct the hybrid nanocomposites of GQD with POXB or POXP to enhance the optical absorption in the light visible region. In order to fully evaluate the possibility of these nanocomposites as a sensitizer of DSSC, we optimize their geometrical structures with the hybrid density functional theory (DFT) methods and confirm the energy stability with frequency analysis. The optical absorption properties are calculated with the time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) method [27]. The results demonstrated that the optical absorption of the nanocomposites is significantly enhanced in comparison to those of the separate GQD, POXB or POXP. After analyzing the energy level and electronic density of LUMO and HOMO, the charge spatial separation and the optical absorption in the visible light range, we identify that seven of the twelve hybrid nanocomposites satisfy the requirement of sensitizer for DSSC. These findings can provide a helpful guide for developing efficient DSSC with the nanocomposites of GQD with POXB, or POXP.

The considered GQDs, POXB and POXP are modeled in Fig. 1 . The GQDs with different sizes are cut from a large graphene sheet. We build up symmetry GQD1(C54H18), GQD2(C66H20), GQD3(C80H22) and GQD4(C96H24). The nanocomposites of the GQDs with POXB or POXP are represented by GQDn-(POXB or POXP) (n = 1, 2, 3, 4). The geometrical structures of the nanocomposites are constructed by attaching the POXB and POXP molecule with the GQDs through an amide linkage. As a result, the POXB or POXP moiety is attached to the GQD through a single CC linkage. The optimized geometries of all the nanocomposites presented in Fig. 2 . The geometrical configurations of the separate GQDs, POXB, POXP and their hybridized nanocomposites are optimized using the hybrid DFT method as implemented in the Gaussian 09 program [28]. The B3LYP [29,30] method with the 6–31 + G(d) basis set [31] is used because the reliability of the theoretical level is confirmed by Chermahini et al. [10]. B3LYP is a gradient-corrected hybrid DFT with exchange-correlation functional in Becke's three-parameter form, which includes a mixture of Hartree-Fock (HF) exchange with the Vosko, Wilk and Nusair (VWN) functional III for local correlation and the correlation functional of Lee, Yang, and Parr (LYP) for non-local correlation. The structure optimizations are performed with a solvent of tetrahydrofuran (THF). The default convergent criteria of Gaussian 09 are used for the energy and force constants. Based on the optimized geometries of the GQDs, POXB, POXP and their hybridized nanocomposites, the optical absorption properties are calculated by employing TDDFT. 40 electronic states are calculated for each nanocomposite with the polarizable continuum model (PCM) [32]. The choice of the exchange correlation functional is important for an accurate description of the charge transfer and electronic properties at the ground and excited state. The absorption spectra for all nanocomposites are calculated at long-range corrected hybrid CAM-B3LYP [33] functionals, with the basis set extension at 6–31 + G(d). The overall energy conversion efficiency (η) that dictates a performance of nanocomposites depends on the short-circuit current density (J SC), the open-circuit photovoltage (V oc), and the fill factor (FF) according to previous investigations [34,35]. The open-circuit photovoltage, V oc, can be calculated based on electron injection from the LUMO level of dye to the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2 (E CBM): [36]
(1)
V oc
=
E LUMO
−
E CBM
e
A high J SC value is associated with an intense light absorption capability and high electron injection efficiency (Φ inject) from the excited state nanocomposites to the E CBM of the TiO2.

J SC is approximated by Eq. (2): [37]
(2)
J SC
= ∫ LHE
λ
Φ inject
η collect
d λ
where LHE (λ) is the light-harvesting efficiency at a specific wavelength, Φ inject is the electron injection efficiency, and η collect is the charge collection efficiency. LHE (λ) can be expressed as:
(3)
LHE = 1 –
10
− ƒ
where f is the absorption intensity of the sensitizer dye. Φ inject is related to the free energy change (in eV) for electron injection onto a titanium dioxide surface that can be obtained from Eq. (4) [38], where E ox nan∗ represents the photoinduced excited states of the nanocomposites and E CBM represents the reduction potential of the TiO2 conduction band. The E ox nan∗ can be estimated by using Eq. (5), where E ox is the redox potential of the nanocomposites in the ground state and E 0–0 is the vertical transition energy.
(4)
Δ
G injec
=
E ox
nan ∗
−
E CBM
(5)
E ox
nao ∗
=
E ox
−
E
0 − 0

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometrical Structures and Vibrational Spectra of QDs the Nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The optimized geometrical structures of all the nanocomposites comprising of GQDs and POXB or POXP are shown in Fig. 2. To evaluate the energy and chemical stabilities of these nanocomposites, the harmonic frequencies and the energy gap (E
g ) between HOMO and LUMO are calculated. The range of the harmonic frequencies, HOMO, LUMO, E
g for all the considered structures is collected in Table 1 . Fortunately, all harmonic frequencies are no imaginary, implying all the structures are stable in energy. In Table 1, the present E
g of GQD1 is 2.809 eV at the B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) theoretical level, which is larger than the 1.96 eV with common DFT but smaller than the 4.68 eV with GW method [39]. We find that the E
g s of GQDs decrease along with the increase of their sizes, except GQD4. The E
g s of POXB and POXP from 2.178 to 2.449 eV, which is roughly the same as the theoretical result at the B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) level in the literature [10]. The E
g s of the nanocomposites are from 1.810 to 2.461 eV, which means that they are moderate chemical stability. The E
g s are also smaller that of the separate GQDs, which may result in the red-shift in its absorption spectra of the nanocomposites. To visually demonstrate the vibrational spectra of the nanocomposites, we plot all the vibrational spectra in Fig. 3 . It is found in the figure that the vibrational absorptions of all the nanocomposites are mainly in the near-infrared region, which implies the sizes of the nanocomposites impact a little on the vibrational spectra. In addition, the nanocomposites can also be used to harvest the solar energy in the near-infrared region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of Nanocomposites as Sensitizers of DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: As a sensitizer of DSSC, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the nanocomposites must satisfy the requirements of the band energy of electrode and the energy level of the electrolyte. Only then can the electrons transfer from the electrolyte to the LUMO of sensitizer and to the electrode. Using the TiO2 electrode and the electrolyte of pair I−/I3 − as the example, we examine the feasibility of the present nanocomposites as the sensitizer. Normally, it is also desirable that the LUMO of the sensitizer locates above the CBM of TiO2 (−4.3 eV) [40], which will ensure the rapid injection of electron from the sensitizer to the electrode. However, the HOMO should be below the redox energy level of I−/I3 − (around −4.80 eV) [41,42], which promises the efficient dye regeneration. The energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO for all the considered nanocomposites, including the CBM of TiO2 and the redox energy level of I−/I3 −are demonstrated in Fig. 4 . The results show that the LUMO levels of all the considered nanocomposites are higher than the CBM of TiO2 and the HOMO levels are lower than the redox energy level of I−/I3 − electrolyte. For example, the LUMO levels of GQD1-POXB (−3.01 eV) is higher than the −4.30 eV of CBM for TiO2, which clearly indicates that the electrons can be injected into the electrode effectively. Moreover, the HOMO energy level of GQD1-POXB (−5.19 eV) is more negative than the −4.80 eV of redox energy level of I−/I3 − electrolyte. Beside the HOMO and LUMO levels, two, other factors can also impact the efficiency of DSSC significantly. One is the efficient electron transfer from the electrolyte to the sensitizer and the other is a spatial separation of the charge carriers on the sensitizer. The effective charge spatial separation can lower the electron-hole recombination rate. Therefore, the obvious spatial charge separation, as a consequence of that, the low chance of recombination of charge carriers is the key factor for the nanocomposites as a sensitizer in DSSC. To demonstrate the spatial charge separation and the contributor and acceptor of the absorption transitions of the nanocomposites, we plot their distributions of HOMO and LUMO in Fig. 5 . The figure clearly indicates that the HOMOs of GQD2-POXB, GQD3-POXB, and GQD4-POXB are localized on GQD, which implicates that the GQD is the contributor of the electron for the optical absorption. Meanwhile, the LUMOs are localized at POXB as the acceptors. Therefore, the obvious spatial charge separation in the optical absorption is identified for the nanocomposites. Unfortunately, this is not true for the remaining nanocomposites, where HOMOs and LUMOs are localized on the same component of the nanocomposites. As a result, the spatial charge separations of these nanocomposites are small, and the recombination of charge carriers will be fast, leading to the low photovoltaic efficiency of DSSC. Therefore, we can conclude that GQD2-POXB, GQD3-POXB, and GQD4-POXB are suitable for sensitizer for DSSC, but the other nanocomposites are not. Compared with GQD1-POXB, we can also find that the GQDs of different sizes have obvious effects on spatial charge separations which is an interesting phenomenon. This means that the spatial charge separation of nanocomposites can be changed by changing the size of GQD. The distributions of HOMO and LUMO for the smallest GQD1-POXB/POXP demonstrate a larger difference from those of the other nanocomposites due to the hybrid coupling interaction is weaker for the small GQD. The energy gap between HOMO and LUMO for the separate GQD1 with the smallest size is larger than those of POXB/POXP, and the HOMO of GQD1 is lower than those of POXB/POXP, which result the HOMO and LUMO of GQD1-POXB/POXP are located on POXB/POXP, not on GQD1. However, the larger GQD has a smaller energy gap and higher HOMO, which makes the HOMO or LUMO of nanocomposites possibly locate on both GQD and POXB/POXP.

Besides the desired band energy of electrode, molecular energy level and charge spatial separation are necessary for the nanocomposites as an efficient sensitizer of DSSC, the optical properties are also important index. The strong optical absorption in the visible light range is necessary to excite the electrons. To obtain the strength of the absorption of the considered structures, we calculated the absorption spectra of all structures by TD-CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d). The calculated oscillator strength (ƒ) of each structure is presented in Fig. 6 , and the corresponding data listed in Table 2 . All of the GQDn-(POXB or POXP) (n = 1, 2, 3, 4) nanocomposites have higher ƒ than those of the separate POXB and POXP. Among these nanocomposites, GQD4-POXP has the highest ƒ with the longest wavelength of 488.28 nm due to the strong hybrid interaction of GQD4 and POXP occurs in the LUMO. The absorption peaks of POXB and POXP occurs at about 430 nm, which is close to that of Chermahini et al. [10] by using TD-CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) level of theory, which implies the present theoretical level is reliable and can be used to calculate the absorption spectra of the other ones. In Fig. 6, an obvious change is observed for the absorption of all nanocomposites in comparison with those of the separate GQD or dye molecules. Compared to the absorption of POXB and POXP, some blue-shift is observed for GQDn-(POXB or POXP) (n = 1, 2, 3). However, GQD4-POXB and GQD4-POXB show red-shifted and the absorption peaks have moved into in the visible light regions, and the strongest optical absorption in the range attribute to GQD4-POXP. This finding implies that GQD4-POXP and GQD4-POXB nanocomposites can effectively respond the solar light and should be the promising candidates to design high-efficiency materials for harvesting solar energy.

To examine the electronic dynamics in the transferring process, we calculate E ox nao∗ , ΔGinjec, LHE, and V oc of all nanocomposites and present them in Table 3 . According to the above discussions, we can conclude that GQD2-POXB, GQD3-POXB, and GQD4-POXB are suitable for sensitizer for DSSC. The results show the ΔGinjec of all nanocomposites are higher than those of POXB and POXP. Therefore, these nanocomposites have a higher driving force of injection electron than those of the dye molecules of POXB and POXP. The E ox nao∗ of GQD2-POXB, GQD3-POXB, and GQD4-POXB are lower but ΔGinjec higher than that of POXB, respectively. The LHE and V oc values of all nanocomposites are significantly changed in comparison with POXB and POXP. The results demonstrate that GQD2-POXB and GQD4-POXB nanocomposites can provide a larger driving force, larger LHE and higher V oc than the single molecules of POXB. Among these two nanocomposites, GQD4-POXB with the larger LHE and V oc and red-shifted absorption could be the best candidate as the sensitizer of DSSC.

In summary, the geometrical structures and electronic properties of eight nanocomposites comprising of four GQDs with POXB or POXP are investigated at B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) theoretical level, while their optical absorption properties are calculated by using TDDFT with CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d). The stable structures of the nanocomposites are obtained by optimization and harmonic frequency analysis. The results indicate that all the nanocomposites are stable in energy and mediate chemical stability. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of all nanocomposites satisfy the requirements of typical DSSC with the electrode of TiO2 and I−/I3 − electrolyte. However, only GQD2-POXB, GQD3-POXB, and GQD4-POXB demonstrate obvious spatial charge separation with which the recombination of their electron and hole are slow, implicating these nanocomposites is beneficial for sensitizer of DSSC. The TDDFT results demonstrate the optical absorptions of GQD4-POXB and GQD4-POXP nanocomposites are significantly enhanced in the visible range and near infrared regions than other six nanocomposites, implying the two nanocomposites are suitable for DSSC in the view of harvesting solar energy. Besides, GQD4-POXB has larger LHE and higher V oc. According to all the considered viewpoints, GQD4-POXB is the most promising candidate as a feasible sensitizer for DSSC among all the eight nanocomposites. These findings are helpful for the design of an efficient sensitizer of DSSC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant No. 11574125 and No. NSFC-11374132, as well as the Taishan scholar project of Shandong Province (ts201511055).
